mainmatter.p: A co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow w \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} of monomorphisms in a category \mathcal {C} is called independent when its pullback is a span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookleftarrow \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } \hookrightarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} in which \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } is initial in \mathcal {C}.

frontmatter.title: Independent sums in categories of monomorphisms

mainmatter.p: Independent sums play an important role in the semantics of local state, as developed by Kammar, Levy, Moss, and Staton and further refined by Polzer and Goncharov. Although these authors did not elucidate the universal property of independent sums, they alude to Alex Simpson’s notion of independence structure — which ought to provide, after taking opposite categories, a suitable abstract interface to the notion of independent coproduct. We will explore a special case of this abstract structure that is more concrete than the general case, but nonetheless allows for a conceptual reconstruction of the ideas of Kammar et al. based on simpler and more intuitive universal properties. Although it is important to be able to give explicit computations, a presentation that requires explicit computations does not scale to verifying the beautiful and intricate constructions of op. cit.

The independent sum of a pair of objects u,v \in \mathcal {C} defined to be the initial independent co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u \oplus v \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}}, when it exists.

The independent complement of a monomorphism \rho : u \hookrightarrow v in a category \mathcal {C} is, if it exists, the greatest monomorphism v \ominus \rho \hookrightarrow v such that the co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ v \ominus \rho \hookrightarrow v \hookleftarrow u \right \} \mathclose {}} is an independent sum.
Question. Would it suffice to simply ask that the co-span is independent, but not universally so?
In an extensive category, any coproduct co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+v \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} is an independent sum.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: As soon as the co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+v \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} is independent, it evidently satisfies the universal property of independent sums, which is weaker than that of coproducts. Coproduct co-spans are always independent in an extensive category.

mainmatter.p: In an extensive category \mathcal {C}, the independent complement of a monomorphism is precisely the Heyting complement of that morphism when it exists.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: This follows immediately from the fact that independent sums are coproducts in extensive \mathcal {C}.

mainmatter.p: Given a category \mathcal {C}, we define \mathbf {Inj} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathcal {C} \right ) \mathclose {}} to be the wide subcategory of \mathcal {C} spanned by monomorphisms. (As any isomorphism is a monomorphism, the obvious construction of the precategory \mathbf {Inj} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathcal {C} \right ) \mathclose {}} gives a category.)
Let \mathcal {C} be an extensive category. Then independent sums are created by the inclusion \mathbf {Inj} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathcal {C} \right ) \mathclose {}} \hookrightarrow \mathcal {C}.
mainmatter.block:
Proof.
The proof of this fact is purely formal, following by several applications of descent.
Given a coproduct co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+ v \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} in \mathcal {C} along with an independent co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow w \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}}, it suffices to check that the universal map u+ v \to w is a monomorphism. Equivalently, we wish to check that the following square is cartesian:
By the descent property for binary coproducts in an extensive category, it suffices to check that each of the following restrictions of that square along the coproduct injections is cartesian:
We will consider only the first square, which we rotate ninety degrees.
By the disjointness and stability of coproducts, we have an isomorphism u \cong { \mathopen {} \left ( u \times _{ u+v } u \right ) \mathclose {}} + { \mathopen {} \left ( v \times _{ u+v } u \right ) \mathclose {}}, and so we may rewrite the diagram above as follows.
We use the descent law for coproducts in an extensive category once more to reduce the cartesianness of the square above to that of the following two squares:
The first square is cartesian because u \hookrightarrow w is a monomorphism. The second square is cartesian because the span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow w \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} is assumed independent.
Thus it follows that u+v \to w is a monomorphism, and so we may define the independent coproduct { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u \oplus v \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} in \mathbf {Inj} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathcal {C} \right ) \mathclose {}} by the coproduct co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+v \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} in \mathcal {C}.

mainmatter.p: The independent sum of a pair of objects u,v \in \mathcal {C} defined to be the initial independent co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u \oplus v \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}}, when it exists.
